segments.s673-g49: کنون is an adverb 'now' at the clause start. رنج 'hardship' is the direct object, followed by the preposition در 'in' and the compound کارخسرو (کارِ خسرو) 'the affairs/work of Khusrow' — note the ezāfe is written without a space in the poem. The verb بریم is final (typical Persian word order) and is first-person plural (hortative/optative or simple future sense: 'let us/ we will bring/relate'); here بردن is used metaphorically as 'bring up/relate'. The subject 'we' is omitted as usual.
segments.s673-g50: بخواننده is the preposition بِـ 'to' attached to خواننده 'reader', giving a dative 'to the reader' (the preposition is proclitic and often written joined in poetry). آگاهی 'information/knowledge' is the noun, modified by the following adjective نو 'new' (Persian normally places adjectives after nouns). The verb بریم repeats at the end in the same person/meaning as above ('we bring' / 'we shall give'), completing the parallel clause.
segments.s673-g1: چوگستهم وبندوی به آذرگشسپ is a temporal/locative preface: چو = 'when', followed by two names (گستهم = Gostaham, وبندوی = Vanduy) and the preposition به + proper name آذرگشسپ. The sequence places the time/participants before the verb (typical in epic Persian), so this whole chunk sets 'when/where and who'.
segments.s673-g2: فگندند مردی سبک بر دو اسپ contains the verb فگندند (they threw/cast) in past tense followed by its object مردی سبک ('a light man' — سبک = light/in weight or lightly equipped) and the circumstantial phrase بر دو اسپ ('upon/over two steeds'). Word order is verb — object — locative; بر is the preposition 'on/upon'.
segments.s673-g3: که در شب به نزدیک خسرو شود is a subordinate/clausal purpose/relative clause introduced by که; در شب = 'at night', به نزدیک + noun means 'toward the vicinity of' or 'near', خسرو is the king's name (Khosrow), and شود is the verb 'become / come' in the present/subjunctive sense here expressing potentiality ('may come/approach').
segments.s673-g4: از ایران به آگاهی نو شود is a short passive-styled clause: از ایران = 'from Iran', به آگاهی = 'to the knowledge/awareness', نو = 'new', and شود = 'become / come to be' (i.e. 'be made known'). The phrase literally says 'a new awareness from Iran comes to be', i.e. 'new news/information from Iran is reported'.
segments.s673-g5: فرستاده آمد بر شاه نو is a simple past clause: فرستاده 'messenger' (subject), آمد 'came' (past), بر شاه 'to/unto the king' (بر used as the direction), and نو modifies شاه as 'the new king'. The default SOV-derived order places the verb before the directional phrase here.
segments.s673-g6: گذشته شبی تیره از ماه نو is a temporal adverbial: گذشته شبی = 'after (a) night had passed' or 'having passed a night', تیره = 'dark', and از ماه نو = 'from/of the new moon' (i.e. a dark night associated with the new moon). گذشته here is a participial/past adjective marking the elapsed night.
segments.s673-g7: ز آشوب بغداد گفت آنچ دید is compact: ز (poetic from/of) introduces source ('from the turmoil of Baghdad'), گفت is the past verb 'said/told' (reporting what follows), and آنچ دید = 'what he had seen' (آنچ = 'that which', دید = 'saw'). The clause is a simple narrative report of speech/recital.
segments.s673-g8: جوان شد چو برگ گل شنبلید is a simile: جوان شد 'he became young/renewed', چو = 'as/like', and برگ گل شنبلید = 'a leaf/petal of the shenbalid flower' (a plant-name used poetically). The verb شد is past tense 'became' and the likening follows it (predicate — comparative phrase).
segments.s673-g25: چوآمد is a fused classical form meaning 'چو آمد' = 'when (he) came' (temporal clause introducer). ببغداد follows as the destination ('to Baghdad') in postverbal position, a common poetic word order. ز + و (here written separately) together mean 'from him / of him' (زو), so ز marks the source and و stands for the pronoun 'him' contracted onto ز; آگهی is the noun 'news/information' governed by ز.
segments.s673-g26: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that / who'), followed by آمد 'came' (simple past). خریدار is used in a figurative sense 'claimer / taker' (not a literal shopper) and تَخت is 'throne' (object of خریدار). مهی is an adjectival epithet modifying تخت ('moon‑like' / 'radiant'), placed after the noun in this poetic order.
segments.s673-g27: همه شهر is the subject ('the whole city / everybody in the city'). ز marks cause ('from / because of') and governs آگاهی 'the news/awareness' as the reason. آرام یافت is a compound verbal phrase: آرام 'calm' as predicate adjective and یافت (past of یافتن) 'found/obtained' meaning 'became calm' or 'found calm'. The word order (subject — cause — predicate) is typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s673-g28: جهانجوی is an epithet/noun 'the world‑seeker' or 'ambitious man' and is the subject of the clause. از introduces the source ('from/out of') and links آرامشان ('their calm' — آرام + possessive suffix -شان) as the cause. کام یافت is another verbal phrase where کام 'desire/satisfaction' with یافت 'found/was obtained' means 'had his desire fulfilled'.
segments.s673-g29: پذیره here is a noun/nominal verbal notion 'reception / being received' used with شدندش (شدند + enclitic ش = 'they received him' in classical/paratactic usage). بزرگان شهر ('the great men of the city') is the agent phrase; word order places the reception concept first for emphasis and the verbal element شدندش follows.
segments.s673-g30: کسی را is the direct object 'someone' marked by را for definiteness/emphasis. که introduces a relative clause modifying کسی. از مهتری بود بهر is an archaic/poetic way to say 'he was of preeminence for/fit for the role' — از (of/from) + مهتری (preeminence / superiority) + بود (past copula 'was') and بهر ('for / to/for the purpose of'). The clause explains why the elders received him.
segments.s673-g31: نهادند is the past tense verb 'they placed/put' (classical past of نهادن). بر is the preposition 'on/before', and پیشگه here functions in a locative/poetic sense 'before / in front of' (prepositional phrase); تخت عاج is 'the ivory throne' (تخت = throne, عاج = ivory). The verb governs the whole locative phrase indicating where they placed the object.
segments.s673-g32: همان is a demonstrative/adjective 'that same / the very', modifying the following noun phrase طوق زرین وپرمایه تاج. طوق 'collar/circlet' and زرین 'golden' form a close noun–adjective unit; وپرمایه (and‑rich/richly endowed) modifies تاج 'crown'. The coordination links two ornament items placed before the throne.
segments.s673-g17: هم is an adverb here meaning “also/even.” او را uses the object/beneficiary marker را after the pronoun to indicate “to him” (classical Persian often places را after pronouns). کنون is the time adverb “now.” چون یکی is a comparative/qualifying phrase “like/as one” and بنده‌ام is the predicate with the first‑person copular ending -ام (“I am a servant”), so the whole segment reads “Even now I am, to him, like a single servant.”
segments.s673-g18: سخن is the noun “speech/words.” هرچ (here) means “whatever.” گوید is the archaic third‑person present (or historic present) “he says” (the provided sense list did not include this verbal sense). نیوشنده‌ام is an active participle/agent noun with the first‑person ending “I am a listener,” functioning as the predicate; together the clause means “I listen to whatever words he says.”
segments.s673-g19: هم اندر زمان = “also / at that time” (هم adding a sense of ‘also’ or ‘too’). داغ دل is a genitival/compound phrase “the burning (dāgh) of the heart,” here an adverbial/qualifying idea describing mood or motive. با سپاه is the accompanying phrase “with an army.” The whole segment sets time, motive, and accompaniment: “also in that time, with a burning heart, with an army….”
segments.s673-g20: بکردار is a poetic contraction of به‌کردار (“in the guise/shape of”); here it qualifies آتش: “in the form of fire.” بیامد is the past/preterite third‑person singular “came” (the older form biāmad). ز راه is the prepositional phrase “from the road/way.” Together: “an army in the form of fire came from the road.”
segments.s673-g21: سپاهی بد = “a bad/hostile army,” with the adjective بد following the noun (classical Persian allows post‑nominal adjectives). از + place names (بردع و اردبیل) marks origin: “from Bardā (بردع) and Ardabil.” Note that بردع here is a place name (the sense list given did not match that reading).
segments.s673-g22: همی‌رفت is the imperfective/past‑continuous “was going” or “advanced” (همی‑ often signals ongoing or habitual past). با introduces accompaniment (“with”), نامور is an adjective “famed/renowned,” and the reduplicated خیل خیل (“host after host” / “in crowds”) is an emphatic pluralizing phrase. The phrase describes the manner/scale of the advance: “advancing with famed hosts, in crowds.”
segments.s673-g23: از ارمینیه marks origin “from Armenia.” نیز is “also/likewise.” چندی before a noun is a quantifier meaning “some/several” (or “a number of”), so چندی سپاه = “several troops” or “a contingent of soldiers.” The segment gives origin and quantity: “also from Armenia several troops….”
segments.s673-g24: همی‌تاخت is past progressive/iterative “were charging” or “rushed” (همی‑ marking ongoing action). چون باد is a simile “like the wind.” با پور شاه = “with the king’s son”; پور is the noun “son” functioning in a genitive/attributive compound with شاه (“son of the king”), here without an explicit ezāfe (common in verse).
segments.s673-g9: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase meaning “thus he said” (چنین = thus; گفت = said, past 3rd person). Everyکو (هرکو) is the indefinite relative “whoever / anyone who,” and زراه خرد is a prepositional phrase “by / along the path of wisdom” with زراه (ز + راه) introducing the route/means and خرد ‘wisdom’ as its head.
segments.s673-g10: بگذرد is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive-form verb here meaning “passes / goes beyond” (a general statement). بتیزی is the noun object (idol-worship / idol-likeness) and ز بی‌دانشی is an ablative/causal phrase “out of / because of ignorance” modifying the action.
segments.s673-g11: نترسد is the negative present (third person) of ترسیدن and here has a hortative/generalizing sense “let him not fear / he should not fear.” ز کردار is an ablative/prepositional phrase “because of / from the deeds,” and چرخ بلند is a noun + adjective (“the lofty Wheel/Heaven”) placed after کردار as the semantic source of those deeds.
segments.s673-g12: شود is the present/future auxiliary/verb “becomes / will become.” زندگانیش is a possessive form meaning “his life” (زندگانی/زندگی + possessive -ش) functioning as the subject/affected entity, and ناسودمند is a predicate adjective “unprofitable, fruitless” describing that resulting state.
segments.s673-g13: گراین is a poetic contraction equivalent to گر این “if this”; بد is the substantive/adjective “bad / evil.” که گفتی is a relative clause “that you said” (که = that; گفتی = you said). خوش‌آمد is a predicate meaning “was pleasing / welcomed,” and مرا (expressed here by the enclitic m + را) marks the experiencer/indirect object “to me.”
segments.s673-g14: خور و خواب is a coordinated nominal phrase “eating (food) and sleeping.” در آتش آمد is an idiomatic-past construction “came into the fire” meaning became tormenting/like burning, with آمد as the verb. مرا again is the clitic marking the experiencer “to me,” attached in poetic word order after the verb.
segments.s673-g15: ولیکن is a contrastive conjunction “but / yet.” پدر is the noun “father.” چون in this context functions as a temporal/adverbial “when / as,” and به خون آخت is a poetic perfective phrase where آخت (from آختن, here meaning “to be mixed/bathed”) takes خون “blood” with به to mean “bathed/steeped in blood.” دست is literal “hand” and begins the next clause (dotted across the line): دست از ایران نکردم = “I did not withdraw my hand from Iran.”
segments.s673-g16: از ایران ندارد/نکردم is the verbal phrase “I did not (take my) hand from Iran” i.e. “I did not abandon Iran”; از + place expresses separation and نکردم is first-person past negative “I did not do / I did not remove.” سران نشست is a short clause “the chiefs / leaders sat” (سران = leaders/chiefs; نشست = sat/held a session), describing their assembly.
segments.s673-g33: بشهر اندرون رفت is a past-tense verbal phrase meaning 'entered into the city' (prep phrase + verb). In this poetic word order the subject خسرو follows the verb (V–S). بدرد is an archaic/contracted form of به درد, here indicating his state ('in pain/distressed') rather than the literal sense 'be useful' — it qualifies Khosrow as he goes.
segments.s673-g34: بنزد + پدر is the prepositional phrase 'to the presence of (his) father' (بنزد = به نزد, an archaic preposition + noun). رفت is the main past verb 'went'. با بادسرد is an adverbial phrase of manner 'with a cold wind' (با = 'with', بادسرد = 'cold wind') modifying how he went.
segments.s673-g35: چه جوییم is a rhetorical/interrogative present-subjunctive ('what shall we seek' or 'what are we to seek'). زین here is an archaic prepositional/demonstrative element meaning 'from this' (followed by the noun it governs). گنبد تیزگرد is a noun + postpositive adjective: 'dome (or vault) swift-turning' (تیزگرد = 'swift').
segments.s673-g36: که introduces a relative clause ('which'). هرگز نیاساید is a negative present form meaning 'never rests' or 'is never at rest' (نیاساید = does not/ cannot rest). از کارکرد is an ablative phrase 'from its action/working' (از = 'from', کارکرد = 'activity, doing/result'), giving the reason the dome does not rest.
segments.s673-g37: This clause should be read as یکی را همی تاج شاهی دهد (the token راهمی here represents را + همی): یکی را = 'to one' (object-marked), همی is the older continuous/aspectual particle, تاج شاهی = 'the crown of kingship' (treat تاج شاهی as the direct object), and دهد = 'he gives' (present/habitual in the poem). Note the object-marker را is attached to یکی and the verb is at the end, a normal Persian order.
segments.s673-g38: یکی را = 'to another/one person'. بدریا literally 'from/into the sea' (بدریا = به دریا/از دریا) functions here as an attributive/location marker indicating an object 'from the sea'. بماهی is an adjectival/epithetic phrase 'moon‑like' (literally 'like the moon'), modifying the implied object (a pearl or gem is implied). دهد is the verb 'gives' closing the clause; Persian often omits a repeated explicit noun (e.g. 'pearl').
segments.s673-g39: یکی را = 'to one' (object-marked). برهنه سروپای is a descriptive phrase: برهنه = 'naked/bare' and سروپای (سروپا) = 'from head to foot' (an idiom meaning 'completely, wholly'). و سفت = 'and stiff/rigid' (سفت coordinating as another adjective). The verb (دهد) is omitted here by ellipsis—the same giving/withholding action continues from the previous clauses.
segments.s673-g40: نه ... و نه is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor'. آرام و خواب = 'rest and sleep'. نه جای نهفت is 'nor a place to hide/shelter'; نهفت is an archaic/literary form related to 'to hide oneself' or 'to be hidden', here used nominally/verbal to express lack of shelter. The clause therefore lists the privations suffered by that one.
segments.s673-g41: یکی را is an accusative/indefinite object (یکی = 'someone') marked by را; را here marks the direct object. دهد is the (narrative) present/indicative 3rd-person singular of دادن 'to give'. توشه شهد و شیر is a noun phrase where توشه 'provision' is followed by a coordinated list of contents (شهد 'honey' و شیر 'and milk'). The whole clause means 'he gives someone a provision of honey and milk.'
segments.s673-g42: بپوشد is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive/jussive form of پوشیدن used here to mean 'he clothes / puts clothing on (him)'; the prefix بـ- is the classical Persian verbal prefix. بدیبا functions as a modifier of the clothing (poetic adjective/adverb 'splendidly'/'marvelous'), followed by a coordinated list of garments خز 'velvet/fur' و حریر 'and silk'. Together: 'he clothes him in splendid brocade, fur and silk.'
segments.s673-g43: سرانجام is an adverb meaning 'at last' or 'finally'. هردو is a pronoun 'both'. بخاک is a fused prepositional phrase (بـ + خاک) meaning 'into/to the ground'. اندرند is an archaic 3rd-person plural copula/locative verb 'they are / are in', so the clause reads 'At last both are in the earth.'
segments.s673-g44: This clause is compact and poetic: بتارک (a prefixed form meaning 'having abandoned / after leaving' or 'by abandoning') introduces the circumstance; بدام is the fused form of به دام 'into the snare' (poetic contraction), and هلاک اندرند uses هلاک 'ruin/destruction' with اندرند 'they are' to mean 'they are in destruction / are ruined.' The sequence describes that, after falling into the snare, they are destroyed.
segments.s673-g45: اگر introduces a conditional clause 'if'. خود here emphasizes the subject ('yourself' / 'you personally'). نزادی is the past/subjunctive form of زادن 'to beget / give birth to' (here 'had begotten'); خردمند is the adjective/noun 'wise' modifying مرد 'man' — together 'a wise man'. So: 'If you yourself had not begotten a wise man...'.
segments.s673-g46: ندیدی is the negative past/perfect of دیدن 'to see' (here 'you have not seen / you would not see'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'in' (here 'in/from the world'), گیتی 'the world'. چنین is a demonstrative/adverb 'such' or 'so', followed by گرم و سرد 'hot and cold' (a phrase meaning extremes/varied conditions). The clause says: 'you would not have seen in the world such hot and cold.'
segments.s673-g47: ندیدی again is 'you have not seen'. جهان 'the world' is the subject. ازبنه is a fused adverbial phrase 'از + بنه' meaning 'from the root / from the foundation' (i.e., utterly/at the base). به here introduces the result/direction 'to' or 'into', and بدی is 'evil/wickedness'. Together: 'you have not seen the world from its root become so evil.'
segments.s673-g48: This is a pair of conditional echoes introduced by اگر (if). که links the clause ('if it is that...'). بدی before مرد is a poetically inverted attributive use meaning 'bad/evil' modifying مرد 'man' (i.e. 'if a man is bad'); the repetition اگر ... اگر gives alternative possibilities — the second if clause has مه which here is archaic/poetic and does not match the ordinary modern senses listed (contextually it behaves like an emphatic/first-person pronoun 'I' or a word meaning 'me / myself' in older usage), so the second clause reads roughly 'or if I am bad.'

sentences.s673-s25: Now we will relate the hardships in Khusrow's affairs, and bring new information to the reader.
sentences.s673-s1: When Gostaham and Vanduy came to Āzar-Gashasp, they flung a light man across two steeds.
sentences.s673-s2: so that by night he might approach Khosrow, and that new news from Iran might be made known.
sentences.s673-s3: A messenger came to the new king, after a dark night of the new moon.
sentences.s673-s4: From the turmoil of Baghdad he told what he had seen; he became young like a petal of the shenbalid flower.
sentences.s673-s13: When news of him reached Baghdad that a claimant to the radiant throne had arrived.
sentences.s673-s14: The whole city grew calm at the news; the ambitious seeker of the world found his desire fulfilled by their tranquility.
sentences.s673-s15: The great men of the city received him — a man who, because of his preeminence, was fit for the post.
sentences.s673-s16: They placed before the ivory throne that same golden collar and the richly endowed crown.
sentences.s673-s9: Even now I am like a single servant to him; whatever words he utters I listen to.
sentences.s673-s10: Also, at that time, with the burning of the heart an army — in the guise of fire — came from the road.
sentences.s673-s11: A hostile army from Barda and Ardabil was advancing, with famed hosts in crowds.
sentences.s673-s12: Also from Armenia several troops charged; like the wind they rushed with the king’s son.
sentences.s673-s5: Thus he said: whoever, by the path of wisdom, passes beyond idol-worship born of ignorance.
sentences.s673-s6: Let him not fear the deeds of the lofty Wheel (Heaven); otherwise his life will become fruitless.
sentences.s673-s7: If that bad thing you spoke pleased me, eating and sleeping became like fire to me.
sentences.s673-s8: But when my father was bathed in blood, I did not withdraw my hand from Iran; the chiefs sat (i.e., they held council).
sentences.s673-s17: Khosrow entered the city; distressed, he went to his father's presence with a cold wind.
sentences.s673-s18: What shall we seek from this swift‑turning dome, which never rests from its workings?
sentences.s673-s19: To one he gives the royal crown; to another he gives a thing from the sea, moon‑like.
sentences.s673-s20: To another he leaves (or gives) nothing but bare from head to foot and stiff; he has neither rest nor sleep nor any place to shelter.
sentences.s673-s21: He gives one a provision of honey and milk, and clothes him in splendid brocade, fur (velvet) and silk.
sentences.s673-s22: In the end both lie in the earth; having fallen into a snare, they are destroyed.
sentences.s673-s23: If you yourself had not begotten a wise man, you would not have seen in the world such heat and cold.
sentences.s673-s24: You have not seen the world from its root turn so evil; whether a man is wicked, or even if I were wicked.
